title: REGULARIZATION
sub: Visual exam summary — every section maps to a real lecturer-quiz question.
source: Source · Tilburg — Introduction to Deep Learning · Lecture 6 (Regularization)

sections.title: WHY REGULARIZE? BIAS, VARIANCE & OVERFITTING
sections.exam: Regularization prevents overfitting & improves generalization; high bias = underfitting, high variance = overfitting — shown by training loss falling while validation loss RISES.
sections.ascii:
train loss : ＼＼＼＼＼＼＼＼＼  (keeps falling)
val   loss : ＼＼＼＼＼／／／／  (falls, then RISES)
                     ▲ stop here
 widening gap between the two  =  OVERFITTING

figures.label: WEB DIAGRAM
figures.desc: Total error = bias² + variance (+ noise). As complexity grows bias falls but variance rises; the best model minimises their SUM, not either alone.

figures.label: WEB DIAGRAM
figures.desc: The wiggly curve threads every training point (memorising noise) instead of the smooth trend — so it generalises badly to new data.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: High bias = darts clustered off-target (too simple → underfit); high variance = darts scattered (too sensitive → overfit).

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Training loss keeps dropping while validation loss turns upward — the widening gap is the textbook diagnostic for overfitting.

sections.title: L1 & L2 — PENALISE THE WEIGHTS
sections.exam: Regularization adds a TERM to the objective that constrains the parameters; L2 (weight decay) shrinks weights, L1 makes them sparse; bigger λ → less capacity → can underfit.
sections.ascii:
objective  =  loss  +  λ · penalty
   L2 (ridge / weight decay):  λ·Σ w²    → shrinks weights toward 0 (small, not zero)
   L1 (lasso):                 λ·Σ |w|   → drives some weights EXACTLY to 0 (sparse)
   bigger λ → stronger constraint → lower capacity → may underfit

figures.label: WEB DIAGRAM
figures.desc: Adding a penalty pulls the fitted function toward a simpler, smoother curve that follows the true trend instead of the noise.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Regularization simplifies the boundary so it stops contorting around individual training points.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: The L1 'diamond' meets the loss at the axes → zeros out weights (sparse); the L2 'circle' just shrinks them. L2 = penalise large weights.

sections.title: DROPOUT — DROP UNITS WHILE TRAINING
sections.exam: Dropout randomly masks units (prob p) during TRAINING and keeps ALL units (scaled) at TEST; it drops units rather than adding an objective term — the go-to overfitting fix.
sections.ascii:
TRAIN:   o  x  o  o  x  o      drop each unit with prob p (new mask every step)
TEST :   o  o  o  o  o  o      keep ALL units, scale outputs to compensate

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: A random subset of units is switched off each step, so no single neuron becomes indispensable — this prevents co-adaptation.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Units are dropped only during training; at test every unit is active and outputs are scaled so the expected activation matches.

sections.title: EARLY STOPPING & DATA AUGMENTATION
sections.exam: Early stopping halts at minimum validation loss — it fights OVERFITTING, NOT vanishing gradients (false-statement trap); augmentation must preserve the label.
sections.ascii:
augment a spoken-audio clip:
  + background noise / slight speed change   → ADEQUATE (label still holds)
  reverse the clip in time                    → INADEQUATE (destroys the spoken word)

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Track the validation loss and stop at its minimum, keeping those weights. Curbs overfitting — it is NOT a vanishing-gradient fix.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Make new training examples with label-preserving transforms (crop, noise, mild speed). Must keep the class meaning — reversing audio in time would not.

sections.title: MODEL CAPACITY — THE OVERFITTING DIAL
sections.exam: Too little capacity → underfit (high bias), too much → overfit (high variance); fix an overfitting model with more dropout + L2 weight decay + data augmentation + reduced capacity.
sections.ascii:
test error
  ＼                       ／
    ＼____           ___／
         ‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾        ← sweet spot
  underfit    just right    overfit
 (high bias)              (high variance)

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Test error is U-shaped in capacity: underfit on the left (high bias), overfit on the right (high variance), the sweet spot in the middle.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Past the interpolation point test error can fall AGAIN — where modern over-parameterised networks operate.

sections.title: BATCH NORMALIZATION (& OTHER NORMS)
sections.exam: Batch norm normalises activations with BATCH statistics → mitigates internal covariate shift → stabler, faster training; layer norm uses ONE example's features (Transformers).
sections.ascii:
ŷ = γ · (x − μ)/√(σ² + ε) + β        μ,σ² = mean/variance over the chosen axis
  Batch Norm : average over the BATCH (per feature)        → needs batch stats
  Layer Norm : average over the FEATURES of ONE example    → per-example (Transformers)

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Each feature is normalised using the mean/variance of the current mini-batch, then rescaled by learnable γ and β.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: As lower layers update, the input distribution to each layer keeps shifting; BN steadies it — the 'internal covariate shift' answer.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Subtract the batch mean, divide by √(variance+ε), then scale (γ) and shift (β); γ, β let the layer undo normalisation if needed.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Batch / layer / instance / group norm differ only in which axis is averaged. Layer norm (per-example, across features) is what Transformers use.
